excerpt: Welcome to the aesthetics mastery show I'm doctor Tim Pierce hi I'm Randy Pierce and we're joined today by as my four-year-old calls her the peel blade that's because we're going deep on injection technique what it is that holes clinicians back and what you can do when you unders…

segments.text: welcome to the aesthetics mastery show I'm doctor Tim Pierce hi I'm Randy Pierce and we're joined today by as my four-year-old calls her the peel blade that's because we're going deep on injection technique what it is that holes clinicians back and what you can do when you understand the components of great injection technique and the principles you can apply to great better and safer results for your patients so what are the foundations that make up good injection technique so injection technique starts with knowing the purpose of your injection and that sounds obvious but it's something you learn when you start training people and I'd love seeing really good injectors going into a training environment and they certainly have to justify everything that they're doing and it actually pulls apart your reasons for why you do what you do and sometimes you have to change them because you realize you're only doing them for idiosyncratic reasons rather than for a

purpose and there's a great book which I forgot the name of which talks about how you should start every action with being really clear on the outcome that you want and for a good injection technique there's a there's an aesthetic result and there's a minimization of complications and it's those two things that then flow on to help you design the rest of your technique so you know what the aesthetic difference is so it might be for example I want definition on the Vermilion border and you then know the complication risk you don't want to have which is I don't want unnecessary bruising and I don't want to include the superior vascular superior labral artery and if those your two driving forces you might also throw in that only caused unnecessary discomfort to the patient so there might be three things that you have to then respect while you try and get the aesthetic result and your position of the filler when you pull your needle out should be primarily achieving that aesthetic result so you need to know a

little bit about the three dimensions of the structure you're injecting or not a little bit a lot in order to design a technique that balances all these things and that's the key thing it's a balancing force you can't go too much into the aesthetic and you can't be too safe where you don't actually do any work so it's a continuous process of balancing these forces which is why it's endlessly tricky you basically have to think about it very heavily and carefully or copy someone's technique has done that for you and once you are clear on your aesthetic goal and you are aware of the kind of complications you might be able to create how do you then decide which technique you use so the short answer is you train with someone that you really respect and you know that clever and you kind of don't need to do all the thinking initially but what I want everyone to get to is the point where you understand the rationale in detail for everything that you do that's the best place to be in because and what you'll see happen with injectors is and I've seen this so many times with people

I've mentored is that they learn all the injections to really high standard and then they go to their first conference and the first guy up on stage completely does it differently and they go oh my god I'm wrong i I've been doing it wrong the whole time he said seven things are different to how I've been doing it and my whole world is fell apart and that happens when you don't understand the rationale so what I'm always trying to get injectors to do is to build that mental model the roadmap the justification for your injection technique is more important than your actual injection technique for becoming a wise injector as opposed to just someone who replicates what other people do that's interesting because part of me is thinking oh yeah I want to be I'm not a clinician myself but I want to be a wise injector another part of me is thinking you know there is something quite nice about a bit paint-by-numbers actually isn't it you feel secure and like someone else told you to do it and that they are really good but let you say you're on this constant hamster wheel of feeling not enough because each new person that comes along you

aren't able to justify it you just have to feel like oh well what was I doing all this time well every injector starts out that way I mean you know we start out in our training facility with people who's probably never injected a face before I know before starting I might have done a few lidocaine injections and a knee but I hadn't really sculpted a face like it's not part of normal injection practice and um and I started out like people copying like word for word what the injector showing me what to do and getting a result and also knowing why they said that you know there's a bit of justification but what happens is as soon as you see someone do something differently realize you only know a very narrow sliver of the complexity of it so I use this analogy a lot for new injectors which is that it's a difference between a map and knowing where you're going or knowing your area so if you think for example about what it's like if you've never been someone someone's trying to direct you to the corner shop and they say go three rows down you'll see the pub on your left turn right and then there the corner shop is 200 yards further from

there yeah most people can get there but as soon as you start in a different place you're immediately lost and that's what happens with new injectors you've been taught one technique without the rationale is that as soon as some aspect changes they're completely lost and they've that's horrible feeling and they quite often will jump to the new the latest technique that they've been taught and say well that's my new technique for now because there they haven't yet formed the deeper rationale behind each angle position depth but that cut that takes time you can do it quicker if you consciously go after it and search for the rationale every injection and that is the foundation of building your own technique that's robust against other people coming in with new ways of doing things so that you're not just doing the newest technique you're doing the best technique that fits with an overall kind of a wise understanding of the face if you're not and I know this from training people the first time that the first thing is you're your anatomical resolution is much lower and what that means is if you picture for example injecting a nasolabial fold and when

people first start they do not see a big difference between I mean I don't how well you can see on camera but if I'm going straight down right near the Eila base versus an angle that's at 90 degrees to the nose towards the Eila base versus one that's maybe slightly angled up they all look the same to the novice injector whereas for me that injection looks really dangerous because it's going it's angled up towards what I know it's underneath here is the infraorbital artery and it's a little bit closer to also where the facial artery becomes the angular artery so there are two big risk factors for that angle that are different to that angle and that only comes from before each injection getting the Anatomy straight in your head and knowing the main things you don't want to hit now it this is quite obvious really if you imagine so just imagine that you're walking along a cliff edge I mean if you're if you can see the edge of the cliff you don't need to be told don't fall over the cliff like it's part of how you calculate your way of

navigating so the last thing you should always do before you inject and I still to this day do this every injection is I'm picturing the dangerous things I don't want to hit and that automatically informs your technique so it starts with the anatomy where and that continuously evolves because you keep becomes add new layers to it but that's the last thing that goes through my mind is where are the arteries where they're likely to be running you can palpate as well which is a really useful thing to add to it often you can feel pulses in some of these areas and then you ask yourself how do I place the filler in such a way so that it doesn't risk blocking this blood vessel and that will if you're thinking clearly enough it will automatically shift some of your angles because you're already thinking as long as your Anatomy is good that if I go in at this angle I'm getting closer and closer to the infraorbital artery and the facial artery which is making it more and more dangerous so that's something that might help is just getting the key anatomy right last thing before you inject and then modifying your technique accordingly the other

thing is you've got to have a shift in your understanding of how you're actually modifying risk because on the lower end of the spectrum the simplistic understanding of risk is if you do the injection and you successfully don't block a blood vessel for example and you successfully get a result that therefore it was a safe injection and that's actually not true because what we're trying to do is pictured the same injection given a hundred thousand times over your career and ask yourself where's the most likely place for this artery and how do I avoid it on average because the chances are if your angle angled up just a few degrees you're going to be a little bit closer to the density of where their arteries if you imagine facial anatomy is overlaying all of your patients altogether so you take every one of their faces I think map of their arteries and stick one on top of the other until there's a hundred thousand of them there'll be a density of artery over particular areas and it won't be the thickness of one artery it'll be ten times thicker than that so what we've got to do is try and choose a technique that's finding

basically a very small low risk area in the broader area and try and always think about getting there because this is not just one technique it's a pattern of behavior you're gonna repeat for the rest of your career and little shifts will modify risk in a way that makes you someone against an occlusion once every twenty years versus once every three years fascinating idea almost like tracing paper just laying them over on top of each other and then avoiding that area what else would you do when you are getting ready to inject so you said that you're already thinking about the mental model of anatomy and thinking about how to give minimize risk what else you do okay so one once you've got the mental model the anatomy clear in your head and that doesn't just mean the two dimensions because that's where most injectors start you start out with the picture from the textbook the next thing is you got to know the depth so where is this artery I'm trying to avoid and mostly if it's coming out of a foramen it's on the bone otherwise they tend to be in the middle layer in the fatty layer is most common with some exceptions so that's the next thing to

know your depth so you've got the two-dimensional and three-dimensional position and then you need to start thinking about your technique now you design your technique first in your head which is I want to go in at this angle because I think over time that's more likely to keep me further away from the artery at this depth and the next thing is going to be about how do you actually deliver that accurately because isn't there's no good knowing that you want to park in a particular parking spot if you basically cack-handed you don't know if you don't know the car at all you're gonna it's the idea is only half of it how are you gonna execute this plan and that comes from it actually starts with your feet often I see injectors battling with a new technique they've often been injecting for years but then they're taught a new technique and it throws them off balance from their feet so you've got to start by being in a position where your feet are stable you should be rooted to the ground that half the time that makes people look more comfortable immediately it's that kind of slanted on one end with most of your weight and through one side of your body that makes everything look unstable and then you shouldn't

stabilize things in as many ways as possible so feet are stable I often will stabilize my pelvis against the bed I'll then maybe leave my elbow against some other part of the bed or the patient's shoulder it's quite good and ultimately the best month the most control you have is when only you're only having to worry about your fingers and maybe your wrists but you're not trying to control your whole arm now you can get and sometimes I will inject it's using my arm but I'm usually tightening it up against my body so that this is stable I'm basically trying to shrink the amount of processing and stabilization I need to just my the parts of my body that are much more finely coordinated and that will give you the ultimate control so now you've got a clear mental model of where you're trying to go and then you're actually trying to get your stabilized so that you've got that control and then the next step is to do it like relatively the size decisively but slowly so I'd sometimes people when they're nervous they do this too quickly because they're they want they don't want to make the group weights and stuff like that so the first thing I would say is

just do this really slowly and then break it into components so often there's each injection technique has several stages if you think about it because you might go through the dermis and then change angle and then slide in all the way through there then you do your aspiration and then you check the patient's comfortable then you do a small injection watch their response then you do the rest of it but it's those the stages also one of the key things and once you once you're ready to inject it's amazing hearing it talks about in this way because obviously if I'm being injected or if I'm even just watching other injectors they either just look like gods doing it or they look a bit awkward but actually there's so much that goes into it isn't they if you think if you break it down like this and actually it scares me a bit because I'm thinking about you know people who let's just say are less than worthy of injecting just whack it in I suppose they probably have some kind of no it's worried that you can see there are injectors who don't have a clear mental model and they're very easy to spot by

someone who does have a clear mental model because you see I mean I've seen a video of a of someone injecting jawlines literally just going along I'll show you on this model and Anatomy isn't quite right but using a needle along the jawline just basically piercing all the way down and bolus sing-along here and right over here you've got the facial artery and she might be lucky for 500 procedures but eventually there's a really high chance you can hit the facial artery and block the whole blood supply to the part of the face and you'll see on Instagram if you google some of the lower end clinics you will actually see them carry out procedures that are terrifying for those of us with a good mental model and yeah it will there are major complications do happen yeah and so coming back to this so you've got yourself now so you in your mental model you've thought how am I going to avoid complications and you've been obviously careful with Anatomy you've then sought to implement your strategy by getting stabilized which I love especially it from a mindset perspective rooting

yourself in the ground with your two feet and even just imagining yourself rigid to the ground can be very good for a mindset absolutely it's what I do and if someone's nervous on the first day of training that's the first thing that makes them feel better is actually and get you get your leg stable pelvis all that makes you feel differently and the other thing is relaxing your shoulders there's often people if you're tense up here it's like you can't it's almost like you're this these fine motor skills are not allowed yet they're all macro because it's the big defensive muscles so you can run away and punch someone that are all taught and actually relaxing that is also the same thing so then you feel firmly planted relaxed in your shoulders and your hands are moving a bit more freely in and you don't have that those course movements but like watching a snooker player yeah after you absolutely right it changes the state of the injector when they feel certain in their stance and relaxed they are there a better injector as well and you think just to confirm you think that can be done even before we gain experience because someone might be watching this thinking we're all right for you you've

been doing it twelve years but do you think that could be done if you make the decision I think it's relevant even for me injecting twelve years on because you know if I'm running late that can put me in an uncertain state and I can try and lean over the patient a bit more and it feels really different and is what normally triggers me is I just stop trying to in fact I'm really good at not rushing these days I went through a phase of learning the hard way that it has a there's always a negative payoff when I say rushing I mean having time on your mind time does not do people who support we will know this time does not come into my mind and I will sometimes run an hour late but that's because I know that you can't get away with having your brain going even ten minutes ahead you need to be in the moment and that means you need to be planted in the moment and that's a way better way to inject and once you make the decision it also feels right it's the right thing to do for your patient what about coming back taking you back to the aesthetic goal so what are you doing now at this moment this is so you've got yourself stabilized and you know you're ready like mental model what are you going to

do to make sure we get a good result that's a really good question because that's actually the next thing that comes in after you after the safe in fact it's the bit that comes in to design the treatment plan is all about the aesthetic and then the actual injections injection technique is about safety but then as soon as you've got your injection technique right you need to go flip back to the aesthetic goal so the aesthetic goal is something you can work on also for years like you have to really I have this theory that we all know what good aesthetics is we know what beauty is but we don't know in a conscious level so one of the goals of a good aesthetic clinician is to constantly trying to drive that to the surfaces what I was doing with sculpting recently trying to learn to sculpt is that you're driving what you know on a primitive level into your conscious brain so that you know logically why it's there and that is that takes years of practice and we there's a constant relationship between the what you know and choose intuitively and what you know academically about beauty and there's you're constantly thinking about that and you get better and better

at it and it's you know I often say to people I didn't know what a nasolabial fold is on my first course and I didn't know why we were treating it was a slight shadow on some 38 year old when it disappeared I'm like yeah she looks better and that was a that's the first that's what you're doing all the time so then you once you've been doing that for long enough you get an instinct for it and I always say actually women have got a head start on this because we do make you do guys do makeup so you're already making creating beauty on your own face and getting that those - to the gut feeling which you get as an injector as well once you've got that lined up in your mind you know what you're aiming for there's a there's a sense that I get like second itch I know I can make this person better looking let me do it mmm and that's the next thing which is you you've kind of started from that point of view then gone to the safety and then the last thing as you're injecting is you're going back into that zone and waiting to see the result that you're looking for so there's a there's mostly it's similar like you get a feeling a certain amount of volume like point two

mils and the nasolabial fold is going to make a good difference and but sometimes you can just you're just monitoring them for that aesthetic difference and you might go a bit more a bit less you might take it out have a look and they make a decision to go back in again and this is something that patients notice as well I know my patients often say to me I feel like an artwork because they can tell that you're in the zone of the aesthetic obviously being safe that you're constantly like looking at the light and shade and making sure that you're getting the result that you need checking it and then making little adjustments if need be tell me then about the because you said that by stabilizing sort of this kind of this part of the body that's fine but then tell me about the hand what's going to show me with the hand the kinds of things that going on well as I said it's that the stabilization particularly from the elbows first and then mostly I'm thinking about risk with most of the injection techniques I know where I want the filler to be and actually that's

relatively I mean you can put if I use the nasolabial fold an example you can put filler in the nasolabial fold in all sorts of ways and that would be reasonable in terms of just purely getting product there but in terms of safety it massively restricts you so that this is why you know you don't try and put filler in across like that because the facial artery runs across that point so that is still the dominant force so it's like the aesthetics tell me where I need the filler to be and that's in three dimensions and some of it's about the mechanistic component of the face like how it moves as well but that's more of a driving force at this point than the aesthetic cuz I've really made the decision with the esthetic before which is we need a little bit of volume here or a little bit of angle on the cheek and so I'm actually being driven more by a safety at this point I'm trying to find that the lowest risk route to put the needle in knowing that I'm picturing that sense that it's a repetitive process I'm going to do thousands of times and picturing the vessels that may

be there cuz I can't see them unlike dr. master who we interviewed I can't ultrasound everyone at the moment although I love that idea so I'm taking an educated guess about the angle of entry that's most likely to minimize lifetime risk so for example if we talk about the tear trough if you want to replace volume not really the tear trough but that the lateral did cheat Junction if I were to add a bit of volume here there's a way of entering here which predisposes me to basically puncturing someone's eyeball and that's happened we've got a case study from someone doing that on a training day where they went in laterally and basically also caused an area of blindness that's a risky angle of entry i mean i don't don't--i comment specifically on that case because I wasn't there but you know the story is something like you're gonna increase the risk even though you might be on the right track aesthetically do this a hundred thousand times and one day you're gonna miss the bone and go straight into the eyeball so let's choose an angle of entry where even if you go too deep you're not gonna

hit something really important so this might be one of the things that changes my angle is I want to place volume here you might use a candle is another way of doing the cannons have their downside to that assuming we're using a needle I want to have a good reason for my angle and my depth in three dimensions so for me it's going to be safer if I'm going underneath orbicularis oculi and just touching the periosteum I might have also felt for the little zygomatic and facial artery which sometimes has an exit point here and even if I can't feel it I'll choose a position that's likely to be slightly more superior and all of this is guiding my eventual position and my angle of entry so that I don't hit the eye and I don't miss the bone and go into the cheek so tell us about aspirating how do you do that yeah that's a really good question because and what I've picked up at one of the reasons that some train is advocating not Spray ting is because it might cause more trauma and this is quite possibly true the first time you ever aspirate and that it's likely to cause for some

people because if it's a bit fiddly that you might actually stick the needle somewhere you didn't mean to and cause an extra bruise or something like that but the main thing for me is if you don't get over that hurdle at some point then years go by and you know aspirating and like I said it with that whole that thinking of your practice as a repetitive behavior that has risk associated with it that's just one other way that you can reduce risk I think it's a significant way I've had probably hundreds of positive aspirations now I'm pretty sure some of them with from arteries I'm very sure actually because you blood is a different color so I'm confident that it reduces risk probably the majority of people are now there's still a bit of debate but I think it's a bit old hat to say there's no evidence when we've all seen the positive aspirations you do hear that so the question is how do you do it without effectively and sensitively without causing unnecessary trauma so let let's think about all the different ways you could aspirate and basically either cause trauma or have a

meaningless ass brain so the first thing is if you're let's say I was injecting the nasolabial fold I don't normally inject like this but for the sake of aspirating I want to show you that if I place a needle in here aspirate then change my position even three millimeters and then inject then that aspiration was pointless your aspiration really only is sensitive to the point at which you aspirate as long as you inject in that point so and then as soon as you move at any direction it's no longer sensitive now that doesn't mean it has zero value because this is something else people say well it only works with the first injection well that's often the most where we put most product so if I aspirate the yellow base come out all the way to the dermis to just underneath the dermis and then do a bit more which I could always aspirate again for and often I would that doesn't mean the first aspiration didn't help so we're reducing risk but only so long as after the aspiration we're completely stable and then you do your injection

your injection as soon as you move a little bit there's no point aspirating you're not checking the spot where you're injecting you you're checking a nearby area and when it comes to arteries you have to be precise where there's no point so that's the first thing is you need to have a stable patient stable hand it's all back down to that stability if all of this is locked in place so I'm stable at my that my wrist I'm stabilized my fingers even the only bit that's moving here I'm actually resting in the case of a periosteal injection you've got one more place to rest which is on the patient's bone basically sounds terrible but doesn't actually hurt that much and then you aspirate that you can see that needle tip is not moving much at all so that's a meaningful aspiration then I can inject and I know that at the point I've aspirated I can inject safely more safely than without aspirating I can say it that way so the other thing you need to practice is how are you actually going to aspirate and this there's a lot of practice and many different ways of doing it one of the key things I've taught this before in different videos is you need to find

a way of aspirating that has a neutral vector across the syringe and what i mean by that is if i pull with this hand against this hand you can see that i'm it's going to be very hard for me to maintain complete new trial it across the syringe because one hand is likely to be slightly stronger than the other and it will move the tip of the needle so you want to find a way of doing it the easiest way is with one hand it's not actually the easiest way but it's the principle is easiest to illustrate that if i'm pulling a thumb out from these fingers the force that is opposing the thumb is the fingers so that the net force is zero it's very i can't pull my hand apart my thumb loop gonna fly off in a different direction so this is one way that you aspirate in a way that makes the net force across the syringe zero so using the same hand as one way to do that in fact it's the way you do this side as well if you're gonna aspirate using your left hand and stabilize with your right hand you still do the same thing you push with one finger and pull with the other finger so i don't know well that can be seen so I'm sure shut you can zoom up on

that process I'm actually pushing the one plastic area away and pulling with the other so the net force across my hand is zero and that is that's the key with a stable aspiration you can see that needle tip is not moving much like and stable as soon as you do that which is what most new injectors do they do that you've now got your stabilizing points the middle of your body is really likely you're going to show some imperfections there and move position and that's really frustrating so keep cause more bruising yeah if you if when you when people first try this if they're not doing it in a way that's neutral they move the needle tip around a needle tip that means it's stabbing into different areas so and this is why it's probably reasonable you know you haven't got a good aspiration technique the argument that it causes more drama is probably true which is why you need to understand a special technique first which is all around the stability and the neutral vector across the syringe that will give you control the next thing I'll just say about aspirating before we move on is there's nothing to stop you aspirating more than once and I certainly do this in more high-risk

areas so I use them maybe if you're doing the Vermilion border over here you know that the superior labral artery runs up in that area and you've had an occlusion there in the past we did this recently for the complications program with someone who had an occlusion there we went through how can you chip away that risk even more so you can aspirate once at the beginning you could do a little linear thread and then you can stop and aspirate again so long as you're using a filler that you know aspirates and we've tested this maybe we can put that link in the bio yeah we did a aspirating test using the various different fillers so we'll drop a link in the description so you know your filler aspirates even when you've injected some filler through the cylinder of the needle and then you can aspirate again give it a second and do a little bit more there's nothing to say you shouldn't aspirate two or three times that's not a hundred percent because you're still moving a little bit but each time you're doing it you're minimizing the risk of having a major portion of that needle in the superior labral artery so aspirate multiple times the more you aspirate the

easier and quicker gets you I often in aspirate so efficiently that you hardly notice it it's a little pullback wait a second there's also one other thing around that and people might find useful if they download the link which is there is this theory that you have to aspirate at least ten seconds or it doesn't count and that is true on the far end of some fillers and some of others you can aspirate for five and nothing comes back and so you need to know your filler and some products most of it for example the range we had the biggest success was the vie cross range I think the tier so we density to were also pretty good and the thinner products tend to aspirate really well so for those you know if you waiting two seconds is probably more than F time most of them if you see in the detail we're kind of a second or less straight from my arms a real blood aspirating straight back with a prime needle so that means you'd have to wait forever and you can do this process relatively quickly keep a close eye on it and look for those tiny little speckles of blood that sometimes you get when you're partially in and then carry on

that way in this you can definitely add layers of safety and it doesn't take forever when you practice it as well as the link in the description for that experiment we did Tim did aspirating where dr. Ahmed literally injected your arm Denis with all the different fillers we also have a download for you especially to company this episode which is all about Tim's top tips for great injection technique so if you could tell us what things do you see injectors doing what mistake do you see them doing the most that you think oh and you kind of want to maybe on you see them on videos or whatever and probably one of the more subtle ones that might be worth thinking about I wouldn't say it's always obvious it's obvious when I've got an injector in front of me and it's not that obvious on videos is depth so the difference if you imagine injecting a Vermilion border one of the most common places people inject enhancer that the difference between a safe and unsafe depth is really hard to see on most video footage it's a matter of

one-and-a-half to two millimeters deeper and you're suddenly in a realm where not every time obviously but I'd say you're probably a hundred times more likely to cause a vascular occlusion in the long arc of so you know there's supposed to be one in fifteen thousand chance of doing it so you still you know what is it 1 in 150 or one whatever my math it important be slightly off but it's but basically you're I'm you're much more likely to do it by being a little bit too deep so I would just get your get ask yourself the question how deep do I really need to be knowing that the superior Volare on in viewer laboratory is usually but not always just under the orbicularis Oris muscle and that's depending on the paper you read it's as little as three millimeters so if you google depth of the superior labral artery the first paper that comes up is actually saying it's the average is three millimeters now that's not very much but it's and it's a needle is nowhere near a millimeter thick so you've got room with a good quality product to place it above that only if you're thinking that way and I can tell
